speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor, Norman Salsitz in Springfield, New Jersey. And we are looking at photographs now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken when I was--when I became bar mitzvah. It was a very important picture for me for a few reasons. As first reason is because-- well, I will mention first who is in the picture. Sitting from left, his name is Noah Hutner. He was the best friend I had. And we went in this time in the same Talmud Torah in [NON-ENGLISH]. Next to Noah, is me, Naftali Saleschutz. Standing from left is David Friedenrich. In the middle is Shloimele Grinstein. And to the right, is Laizer from [NON-ENGLISH]. He was from a different town. Now, the middle one, Shloimele Grinstein, you can imagine how tall he was. That he's the one, he the only one who had to stand on a small chair. He was so brilliant that he was accepted to the world famous Yeshiva in Lublin, which was called Yeshiva [NON-ENGLISH]. This was the highest learning center in the world. And he was accepted without money. And my father always said to me, you see, Shloimele was accepted without money. I wanted to pay they should accept you. But you never-- you couldn't make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us what happened to the boys in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the boys-- all the boys were killed in the same day as my family in the 7th of July in 1942 in Belzec death camps. The only one naturally, the only survivor it's me. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] That David Friedenrich--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is standing on the left. He was very proud for his payots-- side locks. They were so curly. And the reason why they were so curly, he every day in the morning, he had to put his finger in prune juice. They didn't have other remedies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in prune juice, and for this reason, they help being curly a whole day. I tried to imitate him. And as much prune juice I put in in my payots, they never were curly. I had long ones. So finally, I had to put them underneath my ear. Under my ear, they shouldn't hang to my shoulders.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a wonderful story, Norman.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, none of us had ties. We had a piece of black satin because we were so religious. Having a tie was already modern. It was not for us. And we used to call this piece of satin a [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is the-- the piece of material which is cover the arc in every temple is-- it called [NON-ENGLISH]. And this we used to call the [NON-ENGLISH] because it covered our front. But none of us had of tie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The picture was taken in 1933 when I became bar mitzvah in Kolbuszowa. I was born 1920. So 1933, I became bar mitzvah. This picture is from the Talmud Torah in Kolbuszowa. Only-- they alive are only standing from the right. Is David Friedenrich. This is that one who used to use prune juice for his face. Next to him, it's me, Naftali Saleschutz. And next to Naftali Saleschutz is Noah Hutner, who was my best friend. Also a survivor is in middle sitting is Maniek Notowich. He was the youngest, when we were in the camp in Kolbuszowa, he was the youngest of us. He survived. Also survivors are standing from the left, the third one from the left is Moishe-- I don't remember now his name. I will remember, but I don't want-- we don't have too much time. We used to call him Moishe [POLISH]-- [POLISH] means in Polish a baker. Because his father was a baker. Next to him is Usher Leibner. Both of them survived the war in Russia. Moishe is living-- he lives now in Israel. And Usher is living in the United States. The rest of them were all killed. In the same day, they were taken to Belzec. Two of them were with me in the woods, and they were killed in 1943. And they are the first from the left side. His name is--

speaker: interviewer
text: But in which row? In which row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Standing in the first row. Standing the first row--

speaker: interviewer
text: From the corner? With the hat?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the corner. His name is Yankel Laibowich. He was with me in the woods over a year when he was killed. Also--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left, with the tie?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left with the tie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Point to it please.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left with the tie. Yankel Laibowich. Also his brother, Nuhem Laibowich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: The fourth from the top row, Nuhem Laibowich was also with me in the woods. The two were killed when the [NON-ENGLISH], the Polish underground, made a raid. And in this day, they killed 19 of our people and they were two of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph was taken in--

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in 1936. As you can see, some of us wear already ties instead this piece of satin because we were older, and we try to be a little more modern. So by wearing a tie made us already more modern dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Norman, could you please explain what this camera is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This camera was a part of my life. I always--

speaker: interviewer
text: This particular camera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not this particular. This same style, the same--

speaker: interviewer
text: One just like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like this. I always was fascinated with pictures since I was a small boy. When my brother, [INAUDIBLE], announced that he's coming from America for a visit, and he asked what he should bring, what kind of a present, I wrote him, if you bring to me-- if you bring me at present, bring me an-- a [NON-ENGLISH]. Naturally, he didn't know what it meant. So I explained to him in the next letter that this is a camera to make pictures. Now he brought me this camera. Probably he couldn't buy or get a cheaper in a old camera than this. Because it's a very old model. But to me, it was a treasure. When I got this camera, I had a hard time getting film. Because it was not a regular film. It was longer than the normal, and important to get a film for this camera was very hard. So I had to write to Warsaw. Finally I got the films. Imagine me, with long payots, with the long [NON-ENGLISH], and running around having a camera-- which it was not very, very nice for a Jewish boy. Nevertheless, I kept it under my [NON-ENGLISH], and I went and I made pictures. Not for-- not girls, but for the family mostly, and for friends. And this camera I had til the war started. My parents begged me to destroy it, because by Germans, you couldn't have a radio, and you couldn't have a camera. Later, when we went to the ghetto, I took the camera along and I made pictures in the ghetto. Again, my parents begged me. They said, if they get-- Germans, will catch me, the camera, they will shoot me. I kept the camera. Only when we were transported to Rzeszow ghetto, I took a hammer and I broke this camera, which was a part of my life. And even though it's not an expensive camera, but for me it was very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now we're going to see some of the beautiful photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always sorry that I didn't save this camera. But when I came to the United States--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say this camera, you mean the one-- the model just like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: The model just-- well.

speaker: interviewer
text: The camera that you used.

speaker: interviewee
text: The camera-- I was sorry that I didn't save the camera. So when I came to United States, I looked around to buy a camera like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like the one you had destroyed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like the one that I had destroyed. To have to show people, because I have the pictures. Nobody believes that the pictures that I made, I made with such a cheap camera.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I looked around. I couldn't find it. Finally, I saw a camera like this in New York in an antique shop. And they asked $200 for the camera. And I knew $200 is a ridiculous price. So I was-- we visited-- we visited [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Buenos Aires. And I went to a flea market. And I see-- on the flea market, I see a camera. And this is the camera. I asked them how much is the camera. And according to our money, I paid them in their money.

speaker: interviewee
text: According to our money, it was half a dollar. I was very happy. And I gave them the half a dollar. I brought back the camera. And now, I can show off, this is the camera. Not exactly, but the same camera in the pictures I saved a few hundred pictures from this camera.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now we have the pleasure--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in the ghetto. We came into the ghetto in July in 1941 in Kolbuszowa. We moved in to our brother's apartment, Leibush, whose apartment was in the ghetto. And this was in the yard. And the people-- and as you can see from this camera, the picture was a smaller size. I enlarged it. And the picture came out quite good. In the ghetto, I knew when we were in the ghetto, there was a Polish photographer who developed pictures for the German soldiers. I knew him. When I sneaked out from the ghetto, I brought him a film and he developed it for me. And once in a while when I saw a picture with the German soldiers made, and I saw it and it was important. And I still have the pictures. I used to steal them from him because he wouldn't give them to me. He was afraid for the German soldiers. Now here sitting to the left, on the left side, is my brother Leibush's oldest son-- his name was Shulem. He was in this time-- yeah, this picture was taken 1942. Shulem was in this time 12 years old. Standing from the left is his sister Ruza-- or in Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the back or in the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: Standing in the front from the left is his sister, Ruza, or in Jewish Rosa. Next, sitting is my sister, my younger sister, Rachel. And she is holding on her lap Heneh, who is the older son of my sister Liba. Next is sitting on the chair Blimcha, and she's the older girl from my sister Malka. She was, in this time, three years old. Standing the last one on the right side, is Shlomo. He's the third child of my brother Leibush, who had three children-- Shulem, Roza, and Shlomo. In the back row, standing from the left, is my sister, my fourth sister Matla-- Matl. And next to her is the daughter of my older sister, Gela's daughter. Her name is Shaindel. All of them in this picture were killed in the same day in July the 7th, 1942 in Belzec. This is my second sister, Malka-- her married name was Leischer. Again, this picture was taken in 1942 in the ghetto. She was a very beautiful woman. Her face was round. She lost a lot of weight. And she has had two daughters. Standing in front of her is Blimcha. She was in this time three years old. And she's holding on her hand her second daughter, Dubcha. All of them were taken to Belzec the same day as the rest of the family in July 7th, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one second please.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this with Shulem, I forgot, this is a very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute please. Start again.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1942. And it is a very, very meaningful picture to me, and it will be to other people, too. I took it in the ghetto in Kolbuszowa. The boy on the left, his name was Shulem, he's the older son of my brother Leibush. And he became 12 years old. And by the Germans, if somebody was 12 years, he had to wear a band with the star of David. And he knew-- because in this time, he had to go start to work for the Germans. From 12 to 60, every Jew had to work for the Germans. And before he became 12, he asked me, the day he will be 12 and he will wear the band he asked me to make him a picture. Because it was so important. He was so proud. Said he's going to wear a band and he would be already a man. He would be equal to other Jews. He didn't realize the consequences of the band.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this day, he came-- we lived together. And he put on the band. And here is the picture of him sitting in showing the audience with the band that he was so proud. Next to him is Blimcha, the daughter of my sister Malka. Next to her is Heneh, the son of my sister Lliba. And on the right, it's me. How did you take the picture if you're in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- now, I didn't have a self-- how you call it? To make a picture like I have now in the modern-- in the modern cameras. So I put the picture on a barrel, I remember. And they were sitting--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the camera?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the camera, yes. And I asked somebody just to push down this little button. And as you can see, in the last minute I run over to them and I bend down and I am in the picture. When I was a picture, I told them to push it down.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1940 when the Germans gave the order to wear the bands. We all belonged to a youth-- [NON-ENGLISH], this was a youth group. a Zionist youth group. Mostly of the one who are in the picture are the same as you saw the picture before when I-- when we became bar mitzvah. None-- only one survived from this group. I am in the middle standing. And the second one is sitting. His name is Majer Orgul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one? Second one, which one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just below you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in front of me. And the rest were all killed. This picture was taken while we went to work to clean the roads from snow. This was-- we didn't have German guards. Extreme right, this was a Polish man. He was from the municipality. He took us and he was with us all day. He brought us back. And those are all people from Kolbuszowa. I am the second from left with the shovel. And all of us had to wear bands. This is the Kolbuszowa rabbi. His name was Yehil Teitelbaum. He was a man in this time about 75 years old. This picture was taken in April 1942, two weeks before my father was killed. The Germans came in this time, and they picked out a few Jews and made pictures. This picture I didn't take. It was taken by the Germans. They came to his house in the ghetto. They asked him to put on the talis, the prayer shawl. And also to put on the [NON-ENGLISH], what Jews put on to pray. As you can see that this was a staged picture-- not that he went to pray. Because if he would go actually to pray, he will have that thing also on his left arm. But the Germans didn't know. And that he was sitting there and they made the picture. As you can see, the fear in his face. And then when my father was killed, he was on the list. But he was in hiding and they didn't kill him. They killed him July the 7th when it was in Rzeszow, the transport to Belzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they take this? What did they do with this? And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. This picture they took is because they took pictures from a few Jews with long beards in this time. They also took a picture of my father. Now when I was by this Polish photographer who developed for the Germans, and when I saw this picture by the German, I stole it from him. As a matter of fact, I have still the original picture which I took from him. I have it in my possession. That I took it from this German-- from this Polish photographer who developed those pictures. This is Dr. Leon Anderman. He was a doctor in our town before the war. And during the German occupation, he became the old man, the president of the Judenrat. He was a man that he actually sacrificed his life for the Jews. He stood up to the Germans because he was an officer in the Austrian army. And later he was an officer in the Polish army. He was respected by the Jews and by the non-Jews. Before the war, he had nothing to do with the Jews. He was assimilated Jew. But during the war, he accepted this job because he knew that he can speak up to the Germans. And if the Jews would ever have a say, I think that Dr. Anderman should be the first saint by the Jews. He could survive the war-- the Poles wanted to help him. But he said, I cannot leave the Jews by themselves. He was taken to Auschwitz because they said, they told him he's too honest. This is Dr. Anderman when he was taken to Auschwitz. And because he was a physician, they made the mug shots. And they gave him the number. And there is there now. And he was killed-- after a short time, he was killed there. After the war, it took me about five years. And I tried. I knew that he had some documentation in Auschwitz. It took me about five years. And from Auschwitz, I got the three pictures-- what they-- what the Germans made while-- before he was killed. This man's name was Laib Waldlaib. But he had a nickname-- we called him Ukelaib. Ukelieb means-- Laib is his name, Ukel means like a schlemiel. This was taken in November 15, 1940, in Kolbuszowa. Before the war, he used to go around from house to house collecting a few pennies, and for Saturday, a piece of challa. When the Germans came in, and when they saw him, they made from him a hero. They make pictures of him. And they send those pictures to Germany. In even news reels have him in the pictures. And they show that how the Jews, that [NON-ENGLISH] Jews lived in Poland. Here, by the Germans, he worked for the Germans. And he cleaned the toilets. He did all kinds of odd jobs. And he also chopped wood for them. So this picture for Ukelaib is a very, very important picture. This picture was taken in Auschwitz in the beginning of February after the liberation. There was an international commission of the crimes against humanity by Hitler. The head of the commission was Archbishop Hewlitt Johnson. He was the Dean of Canterbury. I was assigned as a member to this commission by the Polish government. We were-- I went around with them three months to all places of killings for all death camps. The first three was in Auschwitz. As you see, this was the only picture made of the entrance to Auschwitz from the inside. all the pictures were made 'arbeit macht frei' from the outside. This picture I took from the inside. The cards are written by my sister Rachel to my brother in Palestine David. Since he left our town in 1933, to 1939, every week she wrote him a card, and as you can see, she tried to push in in the card as much as possible. And she wrote it on a card from my father's business. She wrote everything what happened in our town. Who got married, who died, who cat kittens, whose dog got killed. And once every Saturday, all the Kolbuszowa in Palestine came to my brother. He read this card. And they had a report what happened in our town. I have all those cards from '33 to '39. I got them. My brother in Palestine saved all those cards. When he came over here, he gave them to me. Also please notice, that the handwriting that my sister wrote without a line, without a magnifying glass, is the most beautiful written, the letters, and the beautiful Yiddish, and the beautiful language. This is a pass that I received from [NON-ENGLISH], the man that I told the story that he gave-- he gave the order to shoot me and I gave him coffee. And he let me go. He gave me this pass that I could go around and buy the coffee. So the pass is for Kolbuszowa and the [NON-ENGLISH]-- it means the surrounding neighborhood, and to Rzeszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: The opposite side shows that every month he gave me an extension for this pass. The last extension, I didn't finish. Because in the middle of the month, I escaped. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a letter relating to the Holocaust Museum in Washington, DC. An acknowledgment that the plans for the identity card came from Norman. And I'd like to quote directly from the letter. The entire quote. For the entire development of this idea, of the identity card as portrayed on this video that you see right in front of you, grew out of our meeting at your home, that snowy day over a year ago. I'm happy to send you the first, and so far only, samples we have produced of the card ideas.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, they are based on the life stories of your wife, her sister Celia, and your nephew Shulem. And anyone who has gone to the museum will remember that when you enter, you receive a card telling the story of a victim of the same sex and roughly the same age. And this idea was Norman's. And they are acknowledging and thanking him for it. And this is the article that appeared in the magazine about it. And Norman, do you want to very quickly-- Yeah. This is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the idea come to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The idea came to me because I wanted to make more a personal-- personal feeling for the people. When the people came to the Holocaust, they shouldn't just see pictures from strange people. But if everybody received an ID and he connected himself to the ID, this is a personal feeling. And this is what I had in mind. And this what the accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, I thank you so much Norman for--

speaker: interviewee
text: The card that you see is of my wife, Manya Petrankel. And this was the first card produced in Washington the Holocaust Museum. After this, I gave them 15 or 18 names and cards and pictures from my family which they are all in the museum and they are giving out to people when they are visiting the museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were occasions that people from California or from Florida or from other states call me up and they tell me, do you know, I got your card? I got your wife's card? I got your sister-in-law's card. I got your nephew's card.

speaker: interviewer
text: 20 Seconds.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am very, very glad that they accepted this idea and that's going till today.
